one_liner: A 65-year-old male with heart failure and CKD stage 3 presents with generalized weakness and palpitations 2 weeks after starting spironolactone.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient was started on spironolactone 50 mg daily 2 weeks ago by his cardiologist for heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. He has noticed progressive generalized weakness, muscle cramps, and a sensation of palpitations over the past 3 days. He reports mild nausea but no vomiting or diarrhea. He continues to take his other medications including lisinopril and has been using a potassium-based salt substitute at home per a friend's recommendation. He denies chest pain, syncope, or shortness of breath.
stage2.pmh: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (EF 30%), CKD stage 3 (baseline Cr 1.6, eGFR 42), hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus.
stage2.surgical_history: Coronary artery bypass grafting (5 years ago).
stage2.medications: Lisinopril 20 mg daily, spironolactone 50 mg daily (new, 2 weeks), carvedilol 25 mg twice daily, furosemide 40 mg daily, aspirin 81 mg daily, metformin 500 mg twice daily.
stage2.family_history: Father died of sudden cardiac death at age 60.
stage2.social_history: Retired mechanic. Former smoker (30 pack-years, quit 6 years ago). No alcohol or drug use. Uses potassium-based salt substitute (KCl).
stage2.physical_exam: Elderly male appearing fatigued. Alert and oriented. Heart: bradycardic, irregularly irregular rhythm, no murmurs, distant heart sounds. JVP mildly elevated at 10 cm H2O. Lungs: bibasilar crackles. Abdomen: soft, non-tender, no hepatomegaly. Extremities: 1+ bilateral lower extremity edema. Neurologic: decreased deep tendon reflexes bilaterally, 4/5 strength in all extremities, no focal deficits.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Severe hyperkalemia; life-threatening arrhythmia risk; likely from combined ACEi + spironolactone + CKD + KCl salt substitute

lab_results.result: Peaked T waves, prolonged PR interval (240 ms), widened QRS (140 ms), heart rate 54 bpm with irregular rhythm
lab_results.clinical_significance: Classic ECG changes of hyperkalemia: peaked T waves, prolonged PR, widened QRS — indicates cardiac toxicity requiring emergent treatment
Worsened from baseline; CKD + ACEi + spironolactone impair renal potassium excretion
Elevated, consistent with worsened renal function
Mild metabolic acidosis; acidosis drives potassium out of cells, worsening hyperkalemia
Normal; low magnesium can exacerbate cardiac effects of hyperkalemia
Mildly elevated; relevant for insulin-glucose treatment of hyperkalemia
Mild anemia of CKD; no hemolysis to explain pseudohyperkalemia
Non-anion gap metabolic acidosis; acidosis promotes transcellular potassium shift and worsens hyperkalemia

imaging_results.result: Sinus bradycardia at 54 bpm with peaked T waves (most prominent V2-V4), prolonged PR interval (240 ms), widened QRS (140 ms). No ST elevations or depressions.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: ECG changes represent progressive cardiac toxicity from hyperkalemia and demand emergent treatment

imaging_results.result: Cardiomegaly with mild pulmonary vascular congestion. Small bilateral pleural effusions.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Consistent with chronic heart failure; mild congestion

acceptable_tests: Repeat potassium on fresh non-hemolyzed specimen

stage5.final_clue: Severe hyperkalemia (K+ 7.2) with ECG changes (peaked T waves, widened QRS, bradycardia) in a patient on spironolactone + ACE inhibitor + potassium salt substitute with underlying CKD confirms medication-induced hyperkalemia requiring emergent treatment.

debrief.classic_presentation: A patient with CKD and/or heart failure on RAAS inhibitors (ACEi/ARB + mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist) presents with weakness, muscle cramps, and palpitations. Labs reveal severe hyperkalemia with characteristic ECG changes. Additional contributors may include potassium supplements, salt substitutes, or metabolic acidosis.
debrief.pathophysiology: Potassium homeostasis depends on renal excretion (90%) and transcellular shifts. Spironolactone blocks aldosterone receptors in the distal nephron, reducing potassium secretion. ACE inhibitors decrease aldosterone production by blocking angiotensin II. Together in a patient with CKD (already impaired K+ excretion), they dramatically reduce the kidney's ability to excrete potassium. The KCl salt substitute provides an additional potassium load. As serum K+ rises, the resting membrane potential of cardiac myocytes becomes less negative, leading to progressive ECG changes: peaked T waves → prolonged PR → widened QRS → sine wave → ventricular fibrillation/asystole.
debrief.treatment_overview: Emergent treatment for K+ >6.5 or ECG changes: (1) IV calcium gluconate 10% (10 mL over 2-3 min) to stabilize the cardiac membrane — does NOT lower potassium. (2) Shift K+ intracellularly: IV insulin (10 units regular) with dextrose (25g D50W), nebulized albuterol (10-20 mg), and IV sodium bicarbonate if acidotic. (3) Remove potassium: sodium polystyrene sulfonate (Kayexalate) or patiromer/sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (preferred newer agents), loop diuretics if adequate urine output, or emergent hemodialysis if refractory. (4) Discontinue offending agents: spironolactone and potentially hold ACEi. Discontinue KCl salt substitute. Continuous cardiac monitoring.
high_yield_pearls: ECG changes in hyperkalemia follow a predictable progression: peaked T waves (K+ ~6.0) → prolonged PR and flattened P waves (K+ ~7.0) → widened QRS (K+ ~7.5) → sine wave → VFib/asystole.
IV calcium gluconate stabilizes the cardiac membrane within minutes but does NOT lower serum potassium — it is a bridge while definitive treatments are initiated.

The combination of ACE inhibitor + spironolactone + CKD is a well-known high-risk combination for severe hyperkalemia; potassium must be monitored within 1 week of starting or dose-adjusting either agent.
Potassium-based salt substitutes (KCl) are a commonly overlooked cause of hyperkalemia in patients on RAAS inhibitors — always ask about their use.
Insulin-glucose is the fastest way to shift potassium intracellularly (onset 15-30 min, lasts 4-6 hours); always co-administer dextrose to prevent hypoglycemia.
sources: AHA 2023 Scientific Statement: Management of Hyperkalemia
UpToDate: Treatment and prevention of hyperkalemia in adults (2024)
Palmer BF. Managing hyperkalemia caused by inhibitors of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system. NEJM. 2004;351(6):585-592.

after_stage2_more_likely: Medication-induced hyperkalemia — recently started spironolactone on top of ACEi with underlying CKD; using KCl salt substitute
CKD-related hyperkalemia — baseline CKD stage 3 with worsening creatinine
after_stage2_less_likely: Rhabdomyolysis — no history of trauma, immobilization, or severe exertion
Pseudohyperkalemia — symptoms (weakness, palpitations, ECG changes) confirm true hyperkalemia
after_labs_top3: Medication-induced hyperkalemia — K+ 7.2 with ECG changes in setting of spironolactone + ACEi + KCl salt substitute + CKD
CKD-related hyperkalemia — worsened Cr and acidosis contribute, but the medication combination is the primary driver
Acidosis-exacerbated hyperkalemia — metabolic acidosis (pH 7.31) contributes to transcellular K+ shift
